name: Molybdenum
name_origin: From Greek 'molybdos' meaning 'lead', because the chief ore molybdenite was confused with lead and graphite ores until the 18th century.

discovery.discoverer: Carl Wilhelm Scheele (identified); isolated by Peter Jacob Hjelm in 1781

occurrence.abundance_summary: Molybdenum is the 54th most abundant element in Earth's crust at about 1.2 ppm. It occurs principally in the mineral molybdenite (MoS2), often as a byproduct of porphyry copper mining; powellite and wulfenite are minor sources.
states: trace in seawater and biological systems

compounds.name: Molybdenum disulfide
compounds.summary: The principal ore mineral; widely used as a high-performance dry lubricant and increasingly as a 2D semiconductor material.

compounds.name: Molybdenum trioxide
compounds.summary: A pale yellow oxide used as a precursor for molybdenum metal and as a catalyst in petroleum refining.

compounds.name: Ammonium heptamolybdate
compounds.summary: A water-soluble compound used as an analytical reagent and starting material for molybdenum chemistry.

compounds.name: Molybdenum pentachloride
compounds.summary: A dark green volatile solid used as a precursor in organometallic and catalytic chemistry.

compounds.name: Lead molybdate
compounds.summary: Used as a yellow-orange pigment and in scintillation detectors.

uses: Alloying agent in steels and superalloys to increase strength, hardenability, and corrosion resistance
Catalyst in petroleum refining for hydrodesulfurization (Mo on alumina supports)
Lubricant additive (MoS2) for high-temperature and high-pressure applications
Filaments and electrodes in vacuum tubes and X-ray tubes
Essential trace nutrient in nitrogen-fixing enzymes (nitrogenase) of legume bacteria
reactivity_summary: Molybdenum is a hard, silvery transition metal with one of the highest melting points of all elements. It is resistant to attack by acids in the absence of oxidizers but dissolves in hot concentrated nitric or sulfuric acid, and in alkaline oxidizing melts. It does not react with oxygen at room temperature, but oxidizes above 600 C to MoO3, and reacts with halogens at elevated temperatures.
isotopes_summary: Molybdenum has seven naturally occurring isotopes: 92Mo (~14.65%), 94Mo (~9.19%), 95Mo (~15.87%), 96Mo (~16.67%), 97Mo (~9.58%), 98Mo (~24.29%), and 100Mo (~9.74%, observationally stable double beta-decay candidate). The synthetic isotope 99Mo (half-life 66 hours) is the parent of 99mTc, the most widely used medical imaging radioisotope.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
